questions.explanation: Agenda setting is the theory — originally articulated by McCombs and Shaw (1972) — that mass media does not tell people what to think, but is remarkably successful at telling people what to think about, by choosing which issues receive coverage and prominence. When the media focuses on crime, the public rates crime as a top issue; when it focuses on the economy, economic concerns rise. AP Gov Unit 5 connects agenda setting to priming (media influences which standards voters apply to politicians), framing (how stories are presented shapes interpretation), and media as a linkage institution.

questions.prompt: Powers specifically listed in the Constitution.
questions.explanation: Enumerated (or expressed) powers are the specific authorities Article I, Section 8 grants to Congress — to tax, spend, regulate interstate commerce, declare war, raise armies, coin money, and 17 other specific powers. The 10th Amendment reserves to the states powers not granted to the federal government. AP Gov Unit 1/2 tests enumerated powers against implied powers (from the Necessary and Proper Clause), and McCulloch v. Maryland (1819) is the landmark case on their scope and relationship to federal supremacy.

questions.prompt: Constitutional principle restricting public power through law and rights.
questions.explanation: Limited government means that governmental authority is constrained by law, a constitution, and enumerated powers; government can only do what it is authorized to do and must respect individual rights. It is operationalized through the Bill of Rights, separation of powers, federalism, and judicial review. AP Gov Unit 1 treats limited government as one of the six core constitutional principles, and students often contrast it with the expansive federal authority implied by the Necessary and Proper Clause and the commerce power.

questions.prompt: Principle that political power rests with the people.
questions.explanation: Popular sovereignty is the principle that all legitimate governmental authority derives from the consent and will of the people, not from monarchs or divine right. Embedded in the Preamble's 'We the People' and operationalized through elections, referenda, and constitutional amendments, it is one of the six core constitutional principles in AP Gov Unit 1. Students connect it to social contract theory, the Declaration of Independence, and the ongoing tension between popular will and constitutional constraints (counter-majoritarian judicial review).

questions.prompt: Senate tactic used to delay or block action.
questions.explanation: The Senate filibuster allows senators to extend debate indefinitely to delay or block a vote; ending it requires 60 senators to invoke cloture (Rule XXII). It is not in the Constitution and has evolved from actual extended speechmaking to a 'silent filibuster' requiring merely the threat of one. The filibuster was eliminated for executive nominees in 2013 and for SCOTUS nominees in 2017. AP Gov Unit 2 connects it to bicameralism, checks and balances, and polarization — the 60-vote cloture threshold has made bipartisan cooperation a structural legislative requirement.

questions.prompt: Representative system where voters choose officials to make policy.
questions.explanation: Republicanism is the principle that government authority is exercised by elected representatives accountable to the people rather than by direct popular vote. The Founders chose a republic over pure democracy (Federalist No. 10) because they believed a large republic with filtered representation would better control faction and protect minority rights. AP Gov Unit 1 treats republicanism as one of the six core constitutional principles and tests students on its relationship to federalism, the Guarantee Clause (Article IV, Section 4), and the anti-democratic features of constitutional design like the Electoral College and Senate.

questions.prompt: Two-house legislature with House and Senate.
questions.explanation: Bicameralism divides Congress into the House (435 members, 2-year terms, apportioned by population) and the Senate (100 members, 6-year terms, 2 per state). The Connecticut (Great) Compromise created the bicameral design in 1787 to satisfy both large and small states. AP Gov Unit 2 distinguishes House and Senate rules: the Senate allows filibusters and must confirm presidential appointments; the House must originate revenue bills (Article I, Section 7). Both chambers must pass identical legislation before a bill reaches the president.

questions.prompt: Presidential rejection of a bill.
questions.explanation: The presidential veto (Article I, Section 7) allows the president to reject a bill within 10 days of receiving it, returning it to Congress with objections. Congress may override by a 2/3 vote in both chambers — historically rare. Presidents use the veto threat as much as the actual veto to shape legislation. AP Gov Unit 2 presents the veto as a core check on Congress; students should also know the pocket veto (Congress adjourned, bill dies unsigned) and the invalidated line-item veto (Clinton v. New York, 1998).

questions.prompt: Elastic clause letting Congress carry out enumerated powers.
questions.explanation: Article I, Section 8, Clause 18 — the 'elastic clause' — grants Congress power to make all laws 'necessary and proper' for executing its enumerated powers. Chief Justice Marshall read 'necessary' broadly in McCulloch v. Maryland (1819) to mean 'useful or appropriate,' dramatically expanding implied federal powers. AP Gov students link the Necessary and Proper Clause to implied powers, McCulloch, and the evolving debate over federal vs. state authority in cases ranging from Gibbons v. Ogden (1824) to Gonzales v. Raich (2005).

questions.prompt: Constitutional system that formally selects the president through state electors.
questions.explanation: The Electoral College (Article II, Section 1; modified by the 12th Amendment) assigns each state a number of electors equal to its congressional delegation; 270 of 538 electoral votes are needed to win the presidency. Most states allocate all electors winner-take-all. Four times (1824, 1876, 2000, 2016) the winner did not carry the national popular vote. AP Gov Unit 5 connects the Electoral College to federalism, campaign battleground-state strategy, and ongoing reform proposals including the National Popular Vote Interstate Compact.

questions.prompt: Sample where each person has a chance of selection.
questions.explanation: A random sample is a polling technique in which every member of the population has an equal, known chance of selection, allowing results to be generalized with a calculable margin of error. A typical national poll samples ~1,000 people and achieves a ±3% margin at 95% confidence. AP Gov Unit 4 connects random sampling to scientific polling and the interpretation of polling data; students must recognize the difference between random samples and non-representative convenience samples (online polls, call-in polls).

questions.prompt: Constitutional protections from government action.
questions.explanation: Civil liberties are constitutionally protected individual freedoms that limit government power — speech, religion, press, assembly, petition, due process, and privacy rights. They are primarily found in the Bill of Rights (1791) and applied to the states through selective incorporation via the 14th Amendment. AP Gov Unit 3 distinguishes civil liberties (government cannot infringe) from civil rights (government must ensure equal treatment); students must know which amendments protect which rights and the leading SCOTUS cases applying each.

questions.prompt: Rights people possess by nature, such as life, liberty, and property.
questions.explanation: Natural rights are rights held by all people by virtue of their humanity — classically life, liberty, and property (Locke), rephrased as 'life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness' in the Declaration of Independence. Locke argued government exists to secure natural rights and may be overthrown when it fails. AP Gov Unit 1 grounds the Declaration and social contract theory in natural rights philosophy; the concept connects across units to civil liberties (constitutional protection of natural rights) and the limits of government power.

questions.prompt: The idea that government authority comes from the consent of the governed.
questions.explanation: Social contract theory holds that individuals surrender some freedoms to government in exchange for protection of their remaining rights, and that government derives its legitimacy from the consent of the governed. Locke's version — that government may be dissolved if it violates the contract — directly shaped the Declaration of Independence's justification for revolution. AP Gov Unit 1 uses social contract theory to explain the philosophical foundations of American democracy; it connects to popular sovereignty, limited government, and the amendment process.

questions.prompt: Application of Bill of Rights protections to the states through the 14th Amendment.
questions.explanation: Selective incorporation is the case-by-case process by which the Supreme Court has applied most provisions of the Bill of Rights to the states through the 14th Amendment's Due Process Clause. Beginning with Gitlow v. New York (1925, free speech) and continuing through McDonald v. Chicago (2010, Second Amendment), most rights are now incorporated. AP Gov Unit 3 uses selective incorporation to explain how federalism and civil liberties interact: the Bill of Rights originally bound only the federal government; incorporation extended it to state and local governments.

questions.prompt: Fourteenth Amendment protection against unfair government procedures.
questions.explanation: The 14th Amendment's Due Process Clause — 'nor shall any State deprive any person of life, liberty, or property, without due process of law' — has two dimensions: procedural (fair procedures before government deprivation) and substantive (some liberties so fundamental government cannot take them at all, e.g., right to privacy). It is the vehicle for selective incorporation of the Bill of Rights against the states. AP Gov students must know both dimensions, landmark cases (Mapp v. Ohio, Roe v. Wade/Dobbs, McDonald), and the role of due process in the structure of civil liberties.

questions.prompt: Consistent set of beliefs about government and policy.
questions.explanation: A political ideology is a coherent set of values and beliefs about the proper role of government in society. The American spectrum runs from liberal (active government for equality and social welfare) to conservative (limited government, traditional values, free markets), with libertarian (small government across the board) as a cross-cutting option. AP Gov Unit 4 tests students' ability to apply ideological frameworks to specific policy areas and explain how party platforms, public opinion, and elections reflect ideological competition.

questions.prompt: The process by which people develop political beliefs.
questions.explanation: Political socialization is the lifelong process through which individuals acquire their political beliefs, values, and attitudes. The strongest and earliest agent is family (partisan identification is heavily inherited); schools, peers, religion, media, and major events also shape political orientations. AP Gov Unit 4 distinguishes generational effects (events shaping a whole cohort permanently), lifecycle effects (people growing more conservative with age), and period effects (events shifting all groups simultaneously) as mechanisms of political socialization.

questions.prompt: The meeting-based method for selecting candidates or delegates.
questions.explanation: A caucus is a public meeting-based method of selecting party delegates or nominees, where participants gather and show candidate support through discussion and group alignment. Iowa's first-in-the-nation presidential caucus has historically outsized influence on momentum. AP Gov Unit 5 compares caucuses (time-intensive, favor organized activists, low participation) with primary elections (secret ballot, broader participation) as two competing models of democratic party nomination — both are part of the delegate-selection system leading to national conventions.

questions.prompt: Citizens' attitudes about politics and policy.
questions.explanation: Public opinion is the distribution of individual attitudes and views about government, politics, and policy across the population. Measured by scientific polling, it influences all branches: presidents track approval ratings to gauge political capital, Congress monitors issue polls before votes, and courts — though formally insulated — sometimes consider broad social consensus. AP Gov Unit 4 covers the formation of public opinion (socialization, media, events), its measurement (random sampling, margin of error), and its translation into policy through elections and interest groups.

questions.prompt: Method using representative samples to estimate public opinion.
questions.explanation: Scientific polling uses probability-based random sampling to estimate population opinion within a calculable margin of error (typically ±3% for 1,000-person national polls). It differs from push polls (designed to change opinion), straw polls (self-selected), and convenience samples (unrepresentative). Gallup's accurate 1936 FDR prediction (vs. Literary Digest's 10-million-person failure) established the power of small representative samples. AP Gov Unit 4 tests students on polling methodology, interpreting results, and evaluating poll quality.

questions.prompt: The organization seeking to win elections and control government.
questions.explanation: A political party is an organization seeking to win elections and control government by nominating candidates, mobilizing voters, and organizing governing coalitions. American parties serve as linkage institutions connecting citizens to government; unlike interest groups, they seek to hold office rather than merely influence it. AP Gov Unit 5 traces the historical evolution of American party systems, distinguishes the two major parties' current coalitions and platforms, and explains how winner-take-all elections and the Electoral College perpetuate a two-party system.

questions.prompt: Major shift in party coalitions or voter loyalties.
questions.explanation: Party realignment is a durable, large-scale shift in partisan loyalties among the electorate, producing a new dominant party coalition and party era. Key examples: the New Deal realignment (1932, Black voters, labor, South to FDR Democrats) and the Southern realignment (1964–1994, white Southerners to Republicans following the Civil Rights Act). AP Gov Unit 5 tests students on realignment vs. dealignment (weakening party ties generally), critical elections as triggers, and the implications for governance and representation.

questions.prompt: Protections against discrimination and unequal treatment.
choices: New York Times v. United States
questions.explanation: Civil rights are constitutional and statutory protections guaranteeing equal treatment and prohibiting discrimination based on race, sex, national origin, religion, and other characteristics. Grounded in the 13th, 14th, and 15th Amendments (Reconstruction Amendments) and enforced by statutes including the Civil Rights Act of 1964 and the Voting Rights Act of 1965, they represent an affirmative obligation on government to ensure equality. AP Gov students distinguish civil rights (equal treatment guarantees) from civil liberties (negative limits on government power).

questions.prompt: Fourteenth Amendment rule requiring equal treatment under law.
questions.explanation: The Equal Protection Clause of the 14th Amendment commands that no state shall deny any person 'the equal protection of the laws.' Courts apply rational basis review (economic regulations), intermediate scrutiny (gender), or strict scrutiny (race, fundamental rights) depending on the classification. AP Gov Unit 3 connects the clause to Brown v. Board (1954), Shaw v. Reno (1993), and affirmative action cases — it is both a civil rights protection and a limit on race-conscious remedies.

questions.prompt: Election used to select party nominees.
questions.explanation: Primary elections allow voters to select party nominees for the general election, replacing the 'smoke-filled room' boss control of nominations. Closed primaries restrict participation to registered party members; open primaries allow any registered voter to participate. Post-1968 McGovern-Fraser reforms democratized presidential primaries, dramatically increasing their number and importance. AP Gov Unit 5 connects primaries to party polarization (activists dominate low-turnout primaries, pulling nominees to extremes), incumbency, and voter participation.
